content.problemStatement: Given a prime number **p**, find the number of pairs of integers (**a**, **b**) such that **p** < **a**, **p** < **b** and **ab** is divisible by **(a-p)(b-p)**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of test cases T (1 ≤ T ≤ 5). Then T test cases follow, each test case consists of a line containing one prime integer p (2 < p < 10^12).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output one line containing the required number of pairs of integers (a, b) such that p < a, p < b and ab is divisible by (a-p)(b-p).

solution.approach: The key insight here is to realize that for ab to be divisible by (a-p)(b-p), a and b must have a common factor with p. Since p is a prime number, this common factor can only be p itself or 1. Thus, we can iterate over all possible values of a and b that satisfy the given conditions and count the pairs where ab is divisible by (a-p)(b-p).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * p) per test case, O(T * p) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space used does not grow with the size of the input.
